pattern: Because the array is sorted, two subarrays are equal exactly when they share length and value-content, so distinctness can be enforced by collapsing runs of equal values while counting divisible-sum subarrays.
hints: Two subarrays are the same when their value sequences match. In a sorted array, equal value sequences come from runs of identical values, so identical subarrays differ only by which copies of a repeated value they use.

Compress the array into (value, count) runs. A distinct subarray is determined by a start run with a chosen offset, an end run with a chosen length, and full middle runs; identical content from the same runs must be counted once.

Within a maximal block of one repeated value of length c, the distinct subarrays are by length 1..c (c of them, not c*(c+1)/2). Combine that idea across run boundaries and test divisibility of the sum.

edge_inputs.description: small ascending array, several divisible windows
all equal values, distinctness collapses by length
single element not divisible by k
duplicate values must not double-count identical subarrays
k of 1 means every distinct subarray qualifies
no subarray sum divisible by a large k
mixed runs of repeats and singletons

stress.description: sorted array with frequent duplicates and a small k so divisibility and de-duplication both fire
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(60, size))
    vals = sorted(rng.randint(1, 8) for _ in range(n))
    k = rng.randint(1, 12)
    return [vals, k]
stress.note: kept small because the oracle enumerates distinct subarrays explicitly

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def numGoodSubarrays(self, nums, k):
        # group into runs of equal value
        runs = []
        i = 0
        n = len(nums)
        while i < n:
            j = i
            while j < n and nums[j] == nums[i]:
                j += 1
            runs.append((nums[i], j - i))
            i = j
        R = len(runs)
        # prefix sums over runs (full run sums)
        count = 0
        # A distinct subarray is chosen as: start run index a with taken length sa (1..ca
        # if it is also the end run, else it must extend so sa is the suffix length of run a),
        # end run index b with taken length sb, full runs strictly between.
        # Distinctness: identical value-sequences arise only when the choice of how many
        # copies are consumed at the boundary runs is the same multiset. We enumerate by
        # (a, b) and, for each, the number of items used from run a (1..ca) and run b (1..cb).
        for a in range(R):
            va, ca = runs[a]
            # subarrays fully inside run a: lengths 1..ca, sum = va*len
            for L in range(1, ca + 1):
                if (va * L) % k == 0:
                    count += 1
            mid_sum = 0
            for b in range(a + 1, R):
                vb, cb = runs[b]
                # base sum of full runs strictly between a and b
                # use_a in 1..ca (suffix of run a), use_b in 1..cb (prefix of run b)
                # total sum = use_a*va + mid_sum + use_b*vb
                for ua in range(1, ca + 1):
                    for ub in range(1, cb + 1):
                        total = ua * va + mid_sum + ub * vb
                        if total % k == 0:
                            count += 1
                mid_sum += vb * cb
        return count
